Platinum represents top-tier strategic clients; gold represents high-value clients; silver represents mid-tier; bronze and standard represent lower tiers. Used for resource allocation and service level decisions.

Classification of the client entity type. Corporate includes all business entities; government includes federal, state, and local agencies; individual includes natural persons; prospective includes potential clients undergoing conflict checking.

Current status of ethical conflict checking for this client. Clear indicates no conflicts; conflict identified indicates potential conflict requiring resolution; waived indicates conflict waived by all parties; pending indicates check in progress.
Timestamp when this client profile record was first created in the system. Represents the initial data capture event.
Maximum credit limit approved for this client for Work in Progress (WIP) and outstanding accounts receivable. Used for credit risk management and billing holds.

Highest data protection regime applicable to this clients data. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) for EU clients; California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) for California residents; Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) for healthcare; Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) for payment data; Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) for public company financial data.
Name of the third-party electronic billing platform used by the client (e.g., Serengeti Tracker, CounselLink, Collaborati, Legal Tracker, Brightflag). Null if client does not use e-billing.

Indicates whether the client requires electronic billing submission in Legal Electronic Data Exchange Standard (LEDES) format or through a third-party e-billing platform (e.g., Serengeti, CounselLink, Collaborati).
Date of the first matter or engagement opened for this client. Represents the start of the client relationship with the firm.
Three-letter ISO country code representing the primary legal jurisdiction where the client is domiciled or incorporated. Used for conflict checking and regulatory compliance.
Date when the most recent KYC verification was completed and approved. Used to track KYC refresh requirements per firm policy.

business_glossary_term: Know Your Client (KYC) Next Review Date
description: Scheduled date for the next periodic KYC review and refresh. Typically annual or biennial depending on client risk profile and firm policy.

Current status of Know Your Client (KYC) due diligence and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) verification. Verified indicates completed and current KYC; pending indicates in progress; expired indicates refresh required; rejected indicates failed verification; not required indicates exemption.
Date of the most recent matter opened or activity recorded for this client. Used to identify dormant client relationships.

Indicates whether the client has provided consent to receive marketing communications, newsletters, event invitations, and thought leadership materials from the firm. Required for GDPR and CCPA compliance.
Username or identifier of the system user who last modified this client profile record. Used for audit trail and change tracking.
Timestamp when this client profile record was last modified. Updated whenever any attribute of the client profile is changed.

Free-text field for additional notes, special instructions, relationship history, or other relevant information about the client that does not fit structured fields. May include relationship sensitivities, preferences, or historical context.
Indicates whether the client has published Outside Counsel Guidelines that govern billing practices, rate restrictions, staffing requirements, and matter management protocols.
Standard payment terms for this client expressed in days (e.g., 30, 60, 90). Represents the number of days from invoice date that payment is due.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code representing the clients preferred currency for invoicing and billing (e.g., USD, GBP, EUR, JPY).
Primary email address for client communications and correspondence. This is the main point of contact for general client matters.
Primary telephone number for client contact. Includes country code and extension where applicable.
Number of years that client records and matter files must be retained per firm policy, client agreement, or regulatory requirement. Typically ranges from 7 to 10 years for legal files.
Date when the most recent sanctions screening was performed. Sanctions screening should be performed at client intake and periodically thereafter.

Result of screening against sanctions lists (OFAC, UN, EU, etc.). Clear indicates no matches; match indicates potential sanctions hit requiring review; pending indicates screening in progress; not screened indicates screening not yet performed.

Tax identification number for the client entity. For US clients, this is the Employer Identification Number (EIN) or Social Security Number (SSN); for international clients, the equivalent national tax identifier. Used for tax reporting and compliance.
Unique identifier for the relationship team assignment record.
Foreign key to workforce.practice_group.practice_group_id
Reference to the timekeeper (attorney or professional) assigned to this relationship team role.
Reference to the client entity for whom this relationship team is assigned.
The date on which this relationship team assignment was formally approved by the authorizing partner.
The date on which this timekeepers assignment to this relationship team role ended. Null indicates an active assignment.
Additional free-text notes or comments regarding this relationship team assignment. May include special instructions, client preferences, or coordination requirements.

Free-text explanation of why this timekeeper was assigned to this relationship team role. May reference practice area expertise, industry knowledge, geographic coverage, client preference, or succession planning.
The date on which this timekeeper was assigned to this relationship team role for the client.

Current lifecycle status of this relationship team assignment. Active assignments are operational; Inactive assignments are historical; Suspended assignments are temporarily paused; Pending Approval assignments await partner approval.

Defines the level of billing authority this timekeeper has for matters related to this client. Full Authority can approve invoices independently; Approval Required must escalate; View Only can review but not approve; No Access cannot view billing information.
The expected frequency of proactive client contact for this timekeeper in this role. Used in relationship management planning and client engagement tracking.

FK to client.client_profile.client_profile_id — Relationship teams are assigned TO a client. Without this FK, you cannot determine which partners and BD managers are responsible for which clients — breaking RPE reporting and escalation routing.
Indicates whether this timekeeper is responsible for monitoring and reporting on client satisfaction metrics for this client. Used in client retention and service quality tracking.
Indicates whether this timekeeper has authority to approve conflict check clearances for new matters involving this client. Typically granted to Client Relationship Partners and Supervising Partners.
The timestamp when this relationship team assignment record was first created in the system.

Indicates whether this timekeeper has active cross-sell or upsell targets for this client relationship. Used in Business Development (BD) pipeline management and opportunity tracking in Microsoft Dynamics 365.
The date from which this relationship team assignment becomes operationally effective. May differ from assignment_start_date for planned future assignments.
Numeric priority order for escalating client issues to this timekeeper. Lower numbers indicate higher priority. Used in automated escalation routing for client service delivery.
The geographic region or jurisdiction this timekeeper is responsible for covering within the client relationship. Supports multi-jurisdictional client service coordination.
Indicates whether this timekeeper is the primary point of contact for the client within their assigned role. Used for escalation routing and client communication prioritization.
The most recent date this timekeeper had documented contact with the client in this relationship role. Used for relationship health monitoring and engagement gap analysis.
The timestamp when this relationship team assignment record was most recently updated.
The next planned date for this timekeeper to contact the client in this relationship role. Supports proactive relationship management and follow-up tracking.
Defines the level of client-related notifications this timekeeper wishes to receive for this assignment. Supports communication workflow automation in Microsoft Dynamics 365.

The percentage of origination credit allocated to this timekeeper for this client relationship. Used in Revenue Per Equity Partner (RPE) and Profit Per Partner (PPP) calculations. Sum across all team members for a client should equal 100%.

The specific practice area or legal specialty this timekeeper is responsible for within the client relationship (e.g., M&A, IP, Litigation, Regulatory Compliance). Supports cross-practice coordination.

The specific role this timekeeper plays in the client relationship team. Client Relationship Partner (CRP) is the primary relationship owner; Originating Partner brought the client to the firm; Supervising Partner oversees matter execution; Relationship Manager handles day-to-day coordination; Business Development Manager drives pipeline and cross-sell; Supporting Fee Earner provides specialized expertise.
The percentage of relationship responsibility allocated to this timekeeper for ongoing client management and service delivery. Used in partner performance evaluation and compensation allocation.

The operational system from which this relationship team assignment record originated. Elite 3E for practice management assignments; Microsoft Dynamics 365 for CRM-driven assignments; Intapp Conflicts for conflict-cleared assignments; Manual Entry for ad-hoc assignments.
The unique identifier of this relationship team assignment record in the source operational system. Used for data lineage and reconciliation.
Indicates whether this assignment is part of a formal succession plan to transition client relationship ownership. Used in partner retirement planning and relationship continuity management.
FK to client.client_profile.client_profile_id — Relationship teams are assigned per client — critical for CRP accountability and escalation routing
Unique identifier for the client segment. Primary key.
Identifier of the partner who approved the creation or last modification of this segment definition. Used for audit trail and governance accountability.

Comma-separated list of practice area codes where this segment is applicable (e.g., CORP, LIT, IP, TAX, EMPLOY). Used to filter segment options during matter intake based on practice area. Null or ALL indicates segment applies to all practice areas.
Date when the segment definition was approved by partnership or pricing committee. Used for audit trail and effective date validation.

Name or identifier of the BD team responsible for relationship management, pipeline development, and client acquisition for this segment. Used in Microsoft Dynamics 365 CRM for lead routing and opportunity assignment.
Current number of active clients assigned to this segment. Updated periodically during segment review cycles. Used for segment size analysis and resource planning.

Level of conflict checking rigor required for clients in this segment. Standard for routine matters, Enhanced for complex corporate transactions, Comprehensive for high-profile litigation or M&A. Drives Intapp Conflicts search scope and review requirements.
Timestamp when this segment record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.

Priority level for cross-selling additional practice area services to clients in this segment. High priority segments receive proactive cross-sell campaigns and dedicated BD resources. Used in Microsoft Dynamics 365 CRM for opportunity targeting.
Organizational level required to approve discounts beyond the standard discount percentage for this segment. Ensures pricing governance and margin protection aligned with segment strategy.
Date when this segment definition was retired or superseded. Null for currently active segments. Used to maintain historical segment assignments for billing and reporting purposes.
Date when this segment definition became effective and available for client assignment. Used for historical segment analysis and rate card versioning.
Timestamp when this segment record was last updated. Used for change tracking and data synchronization across Elite 3E, Microsoft Dynamics 365 CRM, and analytics platforms.
Date when this segment definition was last formally reviewed by pricing and BD leadership. Used to track governance compliance and trigger upcoming review notifications.

Minimum annual revenue threshold (in firm base currency) required for a client to qualify for this segment. Used during client intake and annual segment review processes. Null for segments without revenue criteria (e.g., pro bono, government).
Scheduled date for the next formal review of this segment definition. Calculated based on segment_review_cycle. Used for governance workflow automation and leadership calendar planning.

Free-text field for additional context, special instructions, or historical notes about the segment. Used by pricing and BD teams for segment strategy documentation and institutional knowledge capture.

Indicates whether clients in this segment require direct partner involvement in all matters. True for strategic segments where partner relationship management is mandatory. Drives matter staffing and partner time allocation.

Comma-separated list of preferred AFA types for this segment (e.g., Fixed Fee, Capped Fee, Success Fee, Blended Rate, Retainer). Guides pricing discussions during engagement setup. Null indicates standard hourly billing preference.

Indicates whether clients in this segment require AML sanctions screening and ongoing monitoring. True for segments with elevated financial crime risk. Integrated with Intapp Conflicts for automated screening workflows.

Indicates whether clients in this segment require enhanced KYC due diligence beyond standard intake procedures. True for high-risk segments (e.g., foreign government, high-value transactions). Drives conflict checking and AML screening requirements.
Frequency at which this segment definition, criteria, and client assignments are formally reviewed by pricing and BD leadership. Annual is most common; strategic segments may be reviewed quarterly.

Short alphanumeric code uniquely identifying the segment (e.g., GLOBAL500, MIDMKT, GOVPUB, HNWI, STARTUP). Used as business identifier in Elite 3E rate card selection and Microsoft Dynamics 365 CRM pipeline segmentation.

Detailed narrative describing the segment characteristics, target client profile, strategic importance, and business rationale for segmentation. Used by BD teams and pricing committees for segment strategy alignment.

Full descriptive name of the client segment (e.g., Global 500, Mid-Market Corporate, Government & Public Sector, High Net Worth Individual, Start-up & Emerging Growth). Primary human-readable identifier used across all client-facing and internal reporting.

Current lifecycle status of the segment. Active segments are used for new client classification and rate card assignment. Inactive segments are retained for historical reporting only. Under review indicates pending partnership approval for changes. Deprecated segments are being phased out.

business_glossary_term: Service Level Agreement (SLA) Response Time Hours
description: Maximum hours for initial response to client inquiries for this segment. Used to set client expectations and measure service delivery performance. Tier 1 segments typically have shorter response times (e.g., 4 hours) than lower tiers.

Standard discount percentage off standard rates approved for this segment. Applied automatically in Elite 3E rate card selection unless matter-specific override is authorized. Null indicates no standard discount.

Primary client entity type this segment is designed to serve. Corporate for business entities, Government for public sector agencies, Individual for HNWIs and personal clients, Non-Profit for charitable organizations, Mixed for segments serving multiple types.

Target realization rate (percentage of standard rates actually billed and collected) for this segment. Used to measure pricing effectiveness and write-off performance. Typically ranges from 85% to 100%.
Target annual revenue per equity partner for clients in this segment. Used for segment performance measurement and partner compensation allocation. Null for segments without RPE targets.

Hierarchical tier classification of the segment indicating strategic priority and resource allocation level. Tier 1 represents highest-value segments with dedicated partner attention; Tier 4 represents standard service segments.
Unique identifier for this client-service engagement record. Primary key.

business_glossary_term: Client Service Engagement - Legal Service Id
description: Foreign key linking to the legal service offering record. Identifies which service offering is being engaged.

business_glossary_term: Client Service Engagement - Client Profile Id
description: Foreign key linking to the client profile record. Identifies which client this service engagement belongs to.
Reference to the partner (timekeeper) responsible for delivering this specific service engagement. May differ from the overall client relationship partner.
Foreign key to client_profile. Part of the many-to-many relationship.
Current approval and lifecycle status of this engagement. Tracks workflow from initial proposal through active delivery to completion. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.
Specific billing arrangement negotiated for this engagement, which may differ from the services default pricing model.
Reference to the user who created this engagement record.
Timestamp when this engagement record was created in the system.
Date when this service engagement became active and the firm began delivering this service to the client. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.
Reference identifier to the formal engagement letter (LOE) or contract document that governs this service engagement.
Estimated total value of this service engagement in the clients preferred billing currency.
Date when this service engagement expires or was terminated. Null indicates ongoing engagement. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.
Reference to the user who last modified this engagement record.
Timestamp when this engagement record was last modified.

Client-negotiated rate for this specific service engagement, overriding the standard rate defined in legal_service. Null indicates standard rate applies. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.

Detailed description of the scope of this specific engagement, tailoring the general service offering to this clients specific needs and context. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.
Client-specific service level agreement terms negotiated for this engagement, which may differ from standard SLA defined in legal_service. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.
Unique identifier for this client-package subscription record. Primary key.
Foreign key linking to the service package record. Identifies which service package the client has subscribed to.
Foreign key linking to the client profile record. Identifies which client holds this subscription.

Reference to the partner (timekeeper) responsible for managing this specific subscription relationship, including renewals, pricing negotiations, and service delivery oversight. May differ from the clients overall relationship partner when specialized practice area partners manage specific package subscriptions.

Indicates whether this subscription automatically renews at the end of the subscription term without requiring explicit client approval. True enables automatic renewal; False requires manual renewal negotiation. Impacts revenue forecasting and client engagement workflows.

Frequency at which the client is billed for this service package subscription. Determines invoice generation schedule and revenue recognition pattern. May differ from standard package terms based on client-specific agreements.

External reference number or identifier for the legal contract or engagement letter governing this service package subscription. Links to physical or electronic contract documents in the document management system for audit and compliance purposes.
Timestamp when this subscription record was created in the system. Used for audit trail and subscription lifecycle tracking.

Percentage discount applied to the base package price for this client subscription. Captures negotiated discounts, promotional offers, or relationship-based pricing adjustments. Used to calculate package_price from base price and for discount analysis.

Date when this clients subscription to the service package expires or was terminated. Null indicates an open-ended or currently active subscription. Used for renewal forecasting and revenue recognition cutoff.
Timestamp when this subscription record was last updated. Tracks changes to pricing, renewal status, or other subscription terms for audit and change management purposes.

Actual price charged to this specific client for this service package subscription. May differ from the base_price_amount in service_package due to client-specific negotiations, volume discounts, or relationship tier adjustments. Represents the contracted recurring amount.

Current lifecycle status of the subscription renewal process. Active indicates ongoing subscription; Pending Renewal indicates approaching expiration requiring client decision; Renewed indicates successful renewal executed; Cancelled indicates client-initiated termination; Expired indicates lapsed without renewal; On Hold indicates temporary suspension. Critical for revenue forecasting and client retention workflows.
Date when this clients subscription to the service package became active. Marks the beginning of the subscription term and triggers billing cycle initiation.

Number of times services within this package subscription have been utilized or invoked by the client. Tracks consumption for usage-based billing models, capacity planning, and value realization analysis. Incremented when matters are opened under this package or when package services are delivered.
Primary key for vendor
Foreign key to client.organisation.organisation_id
Self-referencing FK on vendor (parent_vendor_id)
Maximum annual spending threshold approved for this vendor without additional authorization.
Vendors bank account number for electronic payment processing.
Name of the financial institution where the vendor maintains their payment account.
Bank routing or transit number for the vendors financial institution.
First line of the vendors primary business address including street number and name.
Second line of the vendors business address for suite, floor, or building information.
City where the vendors primary business location is situated.
Three-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code for the vendors primary business location.
Postal or ZIP code for the vendors business address.
State or province of the vendors primary business address.
Indicates whether the vendor must undergo conflict of interest screening before engagement on client matters.
Timestamp when the vendor record was first created in the system.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for vendor transactions and payments.

Type of diversity certification held by the vendor such as minority-owned, women-owned, veteran-owned, or disadvantaged business enterprise status. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: mbe|wbe|vbe|dbe|lgbtbe|sdvosb|none — promote to reference product]
Date when the vendors current liability insurance policy expires, requiring renewal verification.
Indicates whether the vendor is required to maintain liability insurance coverage as a condition of engagement.
Date of the most recent vendor performance or compliance review conducted by the firm.
Timestamp when the vendor record was last updated or modified.
Date when the current non-disclosure agreement with the vendor expires.
Indicates whether the vendor has executed a non-disclosure agreement to protect confidential client information.
Scheduled date for the next periodic vendor review or recertification.
Free-form text field for capturing additional information, special instructions, or historical context about the vendor.
Date when the vendor was officially onboarded and approved for engagement by the firm.
Preferred method of payment for vendor invoices.
Standard payment terms agreed with the vendor specifying the timeframe for invoice payment.
Indicates whether the vendor has been designated as a preferred supplier with negotiated rates or priority status.
Email address of the primary contact person at the vendor for business correspondence.
Full name of the primary contact person at the vendor organization for operational communication.
Primary telephone number for reaching the vendor contact person.
Assessment of the vendors risk level based on financial stability, compliance history, and service criticality.
Government-issued tax identification number for the vendor entity used for tax reporting and compliance.
Date when the vendor relationship was formally terminated or the vendor status changed to inactive.
Explanation or business justification for terminating the vendor relationship.
High-level categorization of the vendor for spend analysis and procurement reporting.
The externally-known unique business identifier or code assigned to the vendor for reference in procurement and financial systems.
The full legal name of the vendor organization or individual providing services or products to the firm.
Current lifecycle status of the vendor relationship indicating whether the vendor is approved for engagement.
Classification of the vendor based on the type of goods or services provided to the legal practice.
Primary website address for the vendors online presence and service information.
Unique identifier for the Alternative Fee Arrangement record. Primary key.

Foreign key linking to conflict.conflict_check. Business justification: Alternative fee arrangements establish or modify client relationships and require independent conflict clearance, especially for contingency or success-fee structures where the firms financial intere
Reference to the parent engagement letter, retainer agreement, or Letter of Engagement (LOE) that contains this AFA arrangement.

Foreign key linking to court.docket. Business justification: Alternative fee arrangements are matter-specific billing agreements tied to specific litigation dockets. Firms need to track which AFA applies to each docket for budget management, variance analysis,

Foreign key linking to compliance.indemnity_policy. Business justification: Alternative fee arrangements (contingency fees, success fees, budget caps) are underwritten against professional indemnity coverage. Finance and risk teams verify arrangements fall within policy limit

Foreign key linking to intake.intake_fee_arrangement. Business justification: Alternative fee arrangements in executed contracts originate from fee arrangements proposed during intake. Legal operations link these for variance analysis between proposed and final terms, discount

Foreign key linking to intake.letter_of_engagement. Business justification: AFA arrangements reference fee terms specified in letters of engagement. Legal operations validate executed AFA terms against LOE commitments for billing compliance, client expectation management, and
Reference to the legal matter or case to which this AFA arrangement applies, if the arrangement is matter-specific rather than client-wide.
Reference to the attorney who originated the client relationship or matter that led to this AFA arrangement, relevant for origination credit and Revenue Per Equity Partner (RPE) calculations.

Foreign key linking to knowledge.precedent. Business justification: Alternative fee arrangements reference precedent AFA structures and terms. Tracking this relationship enables benchmarking, helps attorneys propose competitive arrangements based on proven models, and

Foreign key linking to service.pricing_model. Business justification: AFA arrangements are instances of pricing models. Links enable alternative fee tracking, realization rate analysis by pricing model, and AFA performance benchmarking. Replaces denormalized afa_type st
Reference to the equity partner or relationship partner responsible for managing this AFA arrangement and ensuring compliance with its terms.
Reference to the client organization or individual who is party to this Alternative Fee Arrangement.

Foreign key linking to risk.risk_register. Business justification: Alternative fee arrangements carry financial risk (budget overruns, scope creep, collection risk) tracked in risk registers for AFA risk assessment, budget monitoring, and profitability management. Es
Reference to the user (typically a partner, pricing committee member, or Chief Legal Officer) who approved this AFA arrangement. Null if no approval required or not yet approved.
The cumulative number of billable hours recorded against this AFA arrangement to date. Used for tracking progress against estimated hours and identifying potential overruns.
FK to contract.agreement.agreement_id — AFA terms are embedded in specific engagement letters/retainer agreements. FK required to link fee commitments to their governing contract.
FK to contract.agreement.agreement_id — Each AFA arrangement is defined within a specific engagement letter or retainer agreement. The fee terms are contractual commitments embedded in the agreement.

The total fee amount agreed under this AFA arrangement. For fixed fee arrangements, this is the total fee. For capped fee, this is the maximum cap. For subscription retainers, this is the periodic fee amount. Null for contingency arrangements where fee is percentage-based.
The date on which this AFA arrangement was formally approved by the designated authority. Null if not yet approved or no approval required.

Indicates whether this AFA arrangement required formal approval by firm management, pricing committee, or conflicts committee before activation. True: approval was required. False: no approval required.
Descriptive name or title for this AFA arrangement, often reflecting the scope or project name (e.g., M&A Transaction Fixed Fee, IP Portfolio Subscription).
Business-facing unique identifier or reference number for this AFA arrangement, used in client communications and billing documentation.

Current lifecycle status of the AFA arrangement. Draft: being prepared. Proposed: submitted to client. Negotiation: under discussion. Approved: accepted by all parties. Active: currently in effect. Suspended: temporarily paused. Completed: scope fulfilled. Terminated: ended early. Expired: term ended. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: draft|proposed|negotiation|approved|active|suspended|completed|terminated|expired — 9 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Indicates whether this AFA arrangement automatically renews at the end of its term. True: arrangement renews automatically unless terminated. False: arrangement ends at effective end date.

The frequency at which invoices are issued under this AFA arrangement. One-time: single invoice. Monthly/Quarterly/Annual: recurring subscription. Milestone-based: invoice upon achieving milestones. Upon completion: invoice at end of matter. As incurred: invoice as work is performed (for capped fee). [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: one_time|monthly|quarterly|semi_annual|annual|milestone_based|upon_completion|as_incurred — 8 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
The single hourly rate applied to all timekeepers under a blended rate AFA arrangement. Null for non-blended-rate arrangements.
The maximum budget or spending limit for this AFA arrangement. For capped fee arrangements, this is the ceiling. For fixed fee, may represent the not-to-exceed amount including disbursements.
The cumulative amount collected (cash received) under this AFA arrangement to date. Used for tracking cash realization and outstanding Accounts Receivable (AR).
The percentage of recovery or settlement amount that constitutes the firms fee under a contingency arrangement. Typically ranges from 10% to 40%. Null for non-contingency arrangements.
The date and time when this AFA arrangement record was first created in the system.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the agreed fee amount (e.g., USD, GBP, EUR).
The maximum amount of disbursements that can be billed under this AFA arrangement, if a disbursement cap applies. Null if no cap or disbursements are included in fee.

Defines how disbursements (out-of-pocket expenses such as court fees, filing fees, expert witness fees) are treated under this AFA. Included in fee: covered by fixed fee. Billed separately at cost: passed through at actual cost. Billed separately with markup: passed through with percentage markup. Capped: subject to separate cap. Excluded: not covered.

The percentage discount applied to standard hourly rates under this AFA arrangement, if applicable. Used for volume discount or relationship-based pricing. Null if no discount or fixed fee arrangement.
The date on which this AFA arrangement expires or is scheduled to end. Null for open-ended arrangements or those tied to matter completion.
The date on which this AFA arrangement becomes effective and billing under these terms begins.
The estimated total number of billable hours required to complete the scope of services under this AFA arrangement. Used for budgeting and resource planning. Null for non-hourly arrangements.
The cumulative amount invoiced under this AFA arrangement to date, across all invoices. Used for tracking revenue recognition and remaining balance.
Indicates whether this AFA arrangement record is currently active and in use. True: active. False: inactive or archived.

business_glossary_term: Legal Electronic Data Exchange Standard (LEDES) Billing Code
description: The LEDES billing code associated with this AFA arrangement, used for electronic invoice submission and compliance with client billing guidelines.
The date and time when this AFA arrangement record was last modified or updated.
Free-text field for additional notes, special terms, or internal comments regarding this AFA arrangement (e.g., negotiation history, client preferences, special billing instructions).

Defines how work exceeding the agreed scope or budget cap is handled. No overage: firm absorbs excess. Client approval required: additional work requires written approval. Automatic billing: overage billed at standard rates. Write-off: firm writes off excess. Renegotiate: parties renegotiate terms.
The hourly rate or fee rate applied to work that exceeds the agreed scope or budget cap, if overage billing is permitted. Null if no overage billing allowed.
Number of days from invoice date within which payment is due under this AFA arrangement (e.g., 30 days, 60 days).

Description of the key performance indicators (KPIs) or success criteria used to measure performance under this AFA arrangement (e.g., matter outcome, time to resolution, client satisfaction score, cost savings achieved).
Number of days prior to effective end date by which either party must provide notice to prevent auto-renewal or to terminate the arrangement. Null if no auto-renewal or no notice requirement.

Narrative description of how financial and performance risk is allocated between the firm and client under this AFA arrangement (e.g., firm bears risk of scope creep, client bears risk of third-party costs).
Detailed description of the legal services, deliverables, and work scope covered under this AFA arrangement. Defines what is included and excluded from the agreed fee.
The percentage uplift or bonus fee applied upon achieving defined success criteria (e.g., winning case, closing transaction). Null for non-success-fee arrangements.

business_glossary_term: Uniform Task-Based Management System (UTBMS) Task Code
description: The UTBMS task code(s) that define the categories of legal work covered under this AFA arrangement, used for standardized activity-based billing and reporting.
Unique identifier for the agreement type. Primary key for the agreement type reference table.

Foreign key linking to service.pricing_model. Business justification: Agreement types have default pricing models that auto-populate during engagement letter generation. Supports template configuration, new matter intake automation, and ensures consistent fee arrangemen

High-level classification of the agreement type by business function: client engagement (LOE, retainer), vendor/supplier (MSA, SOW), internal administrative, regulatory compliance, intellectual property (IP licensing, PCT filings), or dispute resolution (ADR, arbitration).

business_glossary_term: Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Know Your Client (KYC) Required Flag
description: Indicates whether agreements of this type require Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Know Your Client (KYC) checks before execution. True if AML/KYC is mandatory; False otherwise.
Indicates whether agreements of this type require formal approval workflow (e.g., partner sign-off, risk committee review) before execution. True if approval workflow is mandatory; False otherwise.
Indicates whether agreements of this type typically include automatic renewal provisions. True if auto-renewal is standard; False otherwise.
Default confidentiality classification for agreements of this type: public, internal, confidential, highly confidential, or attorney-client privileged (covered by Legal Professional Privilege - LPP).
Indicates whether agreements of this type require a conflict of interest check before execution. True if conflict checking is mandatory (typical for client engagements); False otherwise.
Timestamp when this agreement type record was first created in the system. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.

Standard dispute resolution mechanism for this agreement type: litigation (court proceedings), arbitration (binding arbitration under ICC, LCIA, or other rules), mediation (facilitated negotiation), or negotiation (direct party-to-party resolution).
Default jurisdiction whose laws govern agreements of this type (e.g., New York, England and Wales, Delaware). Null if no standard governing law applies.
Standard number of days required for termination or non-renewal notice for this agreement type. Null if no standard notice period applies.
Standard duration in months for this agreement type. Null if the agreement type does not have a standard term (e.g., perpetual agreements or one-time transactions).
Date on which this agreement type was retired or deprecated. Null if the agreement type is still active. Format: yyyy-MM-dd.
Date from which this agreement type became available for use in the firms contract lifecycle management system. Format: yyyy-MM-dd.

Indicates whether agreements of this type can be executed using electronic signatures (e.g., DocuSign, Adobe Sign). True if electronic signature is legally acceptable; False if wet signature is required.

business_glossary_term: Financial Threshold in United States Dollars (USD)
description: Monetary threshold in USD above which agreements of this type require additional approval or review (e.g., partner sign-off for engagements over $100,000). Null if no financial threshold applies.

business_glossary_term: Fair Reasonable and Non-Discriminatory (FRAND) Applicable Flag
description: Indicates whether agreements of this type are subject to Fair Reasonable and Non-Discriminatory (FRAND) licensing terms, typically for standard-essential patents. True if FRAND terms apply; False otherwise.

business_glossary_term: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Applicable Flag
description: Indicates whether agreements of this type typically involve processing of personal data subject to GDPR. True if GDPR compliance clauses are standard; False otherwise.
Indicates whether this agreement type is currently active and available for use in new agreements. True if active; False if deprecated or retired.
Timestamp when this agreement type record was last updated. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.

business_glossary_term: Legal Electronic Data Exchange Standard (LEDES) Billing Code
description: LEDES billing code associated with this agreement type for electronic invoice submission. Null if no specific LEDES code applies.

Unit of measure for the renewal term length: month, year, or quarter.

business_glossary_term: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) Required Flag
description: Indicates whether a DPIA is required for this processing activity under GDPR Article 35 (high risk to rights and freedoms of data subjects).

business_glossary_term: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) Risk Identification
description: Identification of risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects arising from the processing, as required under GDPR Article 35(7)(c).

business_glossary_term: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) Trigger Reason
description: Reason why a DPIA is required, if dpia_required_flag is true (e.g., systematic monitoring, large-scale processing of special category data, automated decision-making with legal effects).
Date on which the Data Protection Officer was consulted regarding the DPIA, as required under GDPR Article 35(2).
Summary of the DPOs advice and recommendations following consultation on the DPIA.
Email address of the Data Protection Officer for inquiries and consultation.
Name of the Data Protection Officer responsible for overseeing this processing activity and ensuring GDPR compliance.
Type of privacy register entry: Record of Processing Activities (RoPA) under GDPR Article 30, Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) under GDPR Article 35, or combined entry covering both.
Timestamp when this register entry was last modified.
Date of the most recent review of this register entry.
The lawful basis under GDPR Article 6 for processing personal data: consent, contract performance, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.
Detailed explanation of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or third party, if legal_basis is legitimate_interests. Required under GDPR Article 6(1)(f).
Scheduled date for the next review of this register entry.
Description of organizational security measures implemented to protect personal data (e.g., staff training, access policies, data breach response procedures, confidentiality agreements).
Categories of personal data being processed (e.g., identification data, contact details, financial information, employment records, legal case details). Pipe-separated list.
Unique business identifier or reference code for the processing activity, used for cross-referencing with operational systems and audit trails.
Human-readable name of the data processing activity (e.g., Client Onboarding KYC, Matter Document Management, Employee Performance Review).
Detailed description of the purposes of the data processing activity (e.g., client conflict checking, matter billing, employee recruitment, regulatory reporting).
Categories of recipients to whom personal data may be disclosed (e.g., courts, regulators, external counsel, IT service providers, cloud storage vendors). Pipe-separated list.
Business or legal justification for the retention period, including references to applicable statutes of limitation, regulatory requirements, or legitimate business needs.
The time period for which personal data will be retained, or the criteria used to determine that period (e.g., 7 years post-matter closure, duration of employment plus 6 years).
Frequency at which this register entry and associated DPIA (if applicable) are reviewed and updated.
Indicates whether the processing involves special categories of personal data under GDPR Article 9 (e.g., racial/ethnic origin, health data, biometric data, criminal convictions).
Specific types of special category data processed, if special_category_data_flag is true (e.g., health, racial origin, criminal convictions). Pipe-separated list.
Date on which the supervisory authority was consulted, if supervisory_authority_consultation_required_flag is true.
Indicates whether prior consultation with the supervisory authority is required under GDPR Article 36 (when DPIA indicates high residual risk).
Summary of the supervisory authoritys response and any conditions or recommendations provided.
Description of technical security measures implemented to protect personal data (e.g., encryption at rest and in transit, access controls, multi-factor authentication, pseudonymization).
Names of third countries or international organizations to which data is transferred, if third_country_transfer_flag is true. Pipe-separated list of ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country codes.
Indicates whether personal data is transferred to a third country or international organization outside the EEA.
Additional details about the transfer safeguard mechanism, including reference to documentation or approval decisions.

The safeguard mechanism used for third country transfers: adequacy decision, standard contractual clauses (SCCs), binding corporate rules (BCRs), certification, code of conduct, or other approved mechanism.
Unique identifier for the data subject request. Primary key for this entity.

business_glossary_term: Assigned Dpo Officer Timekeeper Id (Foreign Key)
description: Foreign key linking to workforce.timekeeper. Business justification: Data subject requests (GDPR SARs, erasure requests) are assigned to specific DPO team timekeepers for handling. Linking to timekeeper enables workload balancing, response time tracking by handler, and

Foreign key linking to client.contact. Business justification: Data subject requests (GDPR, CCPA) often come from known client contacts. This FK links the request to the contact master when the requestor is a client contact. is_new_attribute=true because contact_

Foreign key linking to compliance.policy. Business justification: Data subject requests are handled according to the firms data privacy policy. N:1 relationship (many requests per policy). Resolves silo for data_subject_request. No columns to remove - request-specif

Foreign key linking to document.legal_document. Business justification: DSR responses documented in formal correspondence: GDPR Article 15 subject access response letter, Article 17 erasure confirmation, Article 21 objection acknowledgment. Required for linking DSR regist

FK to compliance.data_privacy_register.data_privacy_register_id — Data subject requests relate to specific processing activities documented in the RoPA. Linking DSRs to the relevant processing activity enables the DPO to assess scope and respond accurately.

Foreign key linking to compliance.privacy_breach. Business justification: Data subject requests are often filed by individuals affected by privacy breaches (e.g., requesting confirmation of what data was exposed, requesting deletion after a breach). Linking the DSR to the b
Indicates whether the request is being made by an authorized representative on behalf of the data subject (e.g., parent, guardian, attorney, executor).
Indicates whether the data subject has lodged a formal complaint with the supervisory authority (ICO, CNIL, etc.) regarding the handling of their request.
Reference number of the complaint lodged with the supervisory authority, if applicable.
Timestamp when this data subject request record was first created in the system.
Postal address of the data subject. Used for identity verification and postal correspondence.
Internal firm identifier for the data subject (e.g., client ID, contact ID, employee ID) if the individual is known to the firm.
Total number of pages of personal data provided to the data subject in response to the request.
Total number of database records or data entries provided to the data subject in response to the request.
Date on which the data subject request was escalated.
Indicates whether the request has been escalated to senior management, legal counsel, or external advisors due to complexity, sensitivity, or dispute.
Reason for escalating the data subject request, such as legal complexity, high-profile client, or potential regulatory complaint.
Legal exemption applied to withhold or redact certain data from the response, such as Legal Professional Privilege (LPP), third-party rights, or ongoing litigation.
Extended deadline date if an extension has been granted. GDPR allows up to 2 additional months; CCPA allows up to 45 additional days.
Indicates whether the firm has applied for and communicated an extension to the statutory deadline due to complexity or volume.
Reason provided to the data subject for extending the statutory deadline, such as complexity or volume of requests.
Date on which the data subjects identity was successfully verified.

Method used to verify the identity of the data subject making the request. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: government_id|passport|utility_bill|credit_reference|knowledge_based|biometric|existing_client_records — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Status of identity verification process to confirm the requestor is the legitimate data subject or authorized representative.
Timestamp when this data subject request record was last modified or updated.
Indicates whether Legal Professional Privilege exemption was applied to withhold privileged communications between lawyer and client.
Free-text notes and comments regarding the handling of the data subject request, including internal communications, decisions, and special circumstances.
Final outcome of the data subject request after processing and response.
Total number of calendar days taken to process and respond to the data subject request, from receipt to response.
Channel through which the data subject request was received by the firm.
Date the data subject request was received by the firm. Used to calculate statutory response deadline.
Precise timestamp when the data subject request was received, including time zone. Used for audit trail and SLA tracking.
Detailed reason for refusing the data subject request, if applicable. Must be communicated to the data subject with information about complaint rights.

Primary regulatory jurisdiction governing this data subject request (e.g., UK for GDPR/DPA, California for CCPA). ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: GBR|USA|DEU|FRA|IRL|NLD|ESP|ITA|AUS|CAN — 10 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Full name of the authorized representative making the request on behalf of the data subject, if applicable.
Relationship of the authorized representative to the data subject.
Externally-facing unique reference number provided to the data subject for tracking purposes. Format: DSR-YYYYMMDD-XXXXXX.
Detailed description of the scope of the data subject request, including specific data categories, time periods, or systems requested by the data subject.

Current lifecycle status of the data subject request in the DPO workflow. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: received|identity_verification_pending|identity_verified|under_review|in_progress|awaiting_approval|completed|rejected|withdrawn|escalated — 10 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Type of data subject rights request under GDPR/CCPA/DPA. Subject Access Request (SAR) is the most common type.
Date on which the firm provided its response to the data subject request.
Method used to deliver the response to the data subject.
Statutory deadline by which the firm must respond to the data subject request. Typically 30 calendar days from receipt under GDPR, 45 days under CCPA.
Comma-separated list of internal systems and databases searched to fulfill the data subject request (e.g., Elite 3E, iManage, Relativity, email archives).
Indicates whether the request involves personal data of third parties that must be redacted or exempted from disclosure.
Unique identifier for the Data Protection Impact Assessment record. Primary key.

Foreign key linking to risk.risk_assessment. Business justification: DPIAs are specialized risk assessments for data protection under GDPR Article 35. DPIA process feeds enterprise risk assessment for privacy risks, enabling unified risk treatment and ICO consultation

Foreign key linking to compliance.data_privacy_register. Business justification: DPIAs are conducted FOR specific processing activities registered in the data privacy register. N:1 relationship (many DPIAs per processing activity over time as risks change). Resolves silo for data_

Foreign key linking to document.legal_document. Business justification: DPIA outputs documented in formal assessment reports: GDPR Article 35 DPIA report, ICO DPIA template completion, supervisory authority consultation correspondence. Required for linking DPIA register e
Reference to the client matter or engagement for which this DPIA is being conducted, if applicable. Links to the Matter domain for client-facing processing activities.

Foreign key linking to service.practice_area. Business justification: DPIAs are triggered by practice-area-specific processing (automated document review in litigation, AI contract analysis in corporate). Risk assessments are practice-area-contextualized. DPO requires p
Reference to the client organization for whom this processing activity is being performed, if the DPIA relates to client-specific data processing.
Date on which the DPIA was formally approved.
Final approval status indicating whether the processing activity may proceed based on the DPIA outcome.
Name or identifier of the individual who approved the DPIA (typically a senior compliance officer, DPO, or General Counsel).
Date on which the DPIA assessment was completed and submitted for review.
Name or identifier of the individual or team responsible for conducting the DPIA.
Business-facing unique reference number for the DPIA, typically formatted as DPIA-YYYY-NNNN for external communication and audit trails.
Date on which the DPIA assessment process was initiated.

Current lifecycle status of the DPIA in the review and approval workflow. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: draft|under_review|dpo_review|awaiting_authority_consultation|approved|rejected|requires_revision — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Descriptive title of the DPIA identifying the processing activity or project being assessed.
Timestamp when this DPIA record was first created in the system.
Categories of personal data involved in the processing activity (e.g., contact details, financial data, special category data, criminal conviction data). Comma-separated list.
Name of the legal entity acting as data controller for the processing activity under assessment (typically the firm itself or a client).
Description of measures implemented to ensure only the minimum necessary personal data is collected and processed.
Name of any third-party data processor involved in the processing activity, if applicable.
Categories of individuals whose personal data is being processed (e.g., clients, employees, opposing parties, witnesses, beneficiaries). Comma-separated list.
Indicates whether data subjects or their representatives were consulted regarding the processing, where appropriate.
Summary of advice and recommendations provided by the DPO during consultation.
Date on which the DPO was consulted regarding the DPIA.
Indicates whether the Data Protection Officer was consulted during the DPIA process as required under GDPR Article 35(2).
Timestamp when this DPIA record was most recently modified.
Date on which the DPIA was most recently reviewed and updated.
The lawful basis under GDPR Article 6 that justifies the processing activity.
Description of technical and organizational measures implemented to mitigate identified risks, including safeguards, security measures, and mechanisms to ensure protection of personal data.
Assessment of whether the processing is necessary to achieve the stated purpose, and whether the purpose could be achieved through less intrusive means.
Scheduled date for the next review of this DPIA.
The specific business or legal purpose for which the personal data is being processed (e.g., client matter management, litigation support, regulatory compliance, employee performance management).
Assessment of whether the processing is proportionate to the purpose, considering the rights and freedoms of data subjects and the benefits to the controller and society.
Assessed risk level remaining after mitigation measures have been applied.
The period for which personal data will be retained, or the criteria used to determine that period.
Scheduled frequency for reviewing and updating the DPIA to ensure it remains current and accurate.
Summary of identified risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects arising from the processing activity, including likelihood and severity assessment.
Overall assessed risk level to data subjects after considering likelihood and severity of potential impacts.

Indicates whether the processing involves special category data as defined under GDPR Article 9 (racial/ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership, genetic data, biometric data, health data, sex life, or sexual orientation).
Date on which the supervisory authority was consulted, if required.
Indicates whether prior consultation with the supervisory authority is required because the DPIA indicates high residual risk that cannot be adequately mitigated.
Summary of the decision or guidance provided by the supervisory authority following consultation.
Reference number or identifier assigned by the supervisory authority for the consultation, if applicable.

The specific criterion under GDPR Article 35(3) or supervisory authority guidance that triggered the requirement to conduct this DPIA. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: systematic_monitoring|automated_decision_making|large_scale_special_category|large_scale_criminal_data|innovative_technology|data_matching|profiling|biometric_data|genetic_data|location_tracking|vulnerable_data_subjects|cross_border_transfer — 12 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Indicates whether the processing involves vulnerable data subjects such as children, elderly, mentally incapacitated individuals, or employees in an imbalanced power relationship.
Unique identifier for the professional indemnity insurance claim record. Primary key for the indemnity claim entity.
Reference to the partner who had primary responsibility for the matter at the time of the alleged negligence.

Foreign key linking to compliance.indemnity_policy. Business justification: Claims are made UNDER a policy. N:1 relationship (many claims per policy). indemnity_claim has policy_number and policy_year attributes that reference indemnity_policy. Adding FK resolves silo for ind

Foreign key linking to document.legal_document. Business justification: Claim notifications, coverage opinions, settlement agreements are legal documents: insurer notification letter, coverage counsel opinion, settlement deed, SRA report of claim. Required for linking ind
Reference to the matter that is the subject of the alleged negligence or professional liability claim.

Foreign key linking to service.practice_area. Business justification: Professional indemnity claims arise from practice-area-specific work. Underwriters price coverage by practice area risk profile. Essential for practice-area-level claims analysis, reserve setting, and
Reference to the fee earner (associate, solicitor, or other timekeeper) who performed the work that is the subject of the alleged negligence.
Reference to the client who is the claimant or on whose matter the alleged negligence occurred.
Detailed narrative description of the alleged professional negligence, error, omission, or breach of duty that forms the basis of the claim. Includes circumstances, timeline, and alleged impact.
Date on which the claim was formally closed by the insurer and the firm, either through settlement, withdrawal, or dismissal.
External claim reference number assigned by the insurer or the firms risk management system. Used for correspondence with insurers and tracking across systems.

Current lifecycle status of the indemnity claim. Tracks progression from initial notification through investigation, settlement, and closure. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: notified|acknowledged|investigating|reserved|settled|closed|withdrawn — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

Classification of the nature of the professional indemnity claim based on the type of alleged error or omission. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: negligence|breach_of_duty|conflict_of_interest|missed_deadline|inadequate_advice|document_error|fraud|other — 8 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Primary email address for correspondence with the claimant or their legal representative.
Primary telephone number for the claimant or their legal representative.
Full name of the individual or organization making the claim against the firm. May be the client or a third party affected by the alleged negligence.
Classification of the claimants relationship to the firm and the matter in question.
Name of the individual at the insurer or third-party administrator responsible for managing this claim.
Reason for closing the claim, indicating the final outcome or disposition.
Date on which the insurer confirmed or denied coverage for the claim.
Status of the insurers determination regarding whether the claim is covered under the professional indemnity policy.
Timestamp when this indemnity claim record was first created in the system.
Total costs incurred in defending the claim, including legal fees, expert witness fees, and other litigation expenses. May be covered by the insurer depending on policy terms.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the defence costs amount.
Initial estimate of the firms potential financial exposure or liability for this claim, including damages, costs, and interest. Updated as the claim progresses.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the estimated exposure amount.
Date on which the alleged negligent act, error, or omission occurred or was discovered.
Date on which the insurer formally acknowledged receipt of the claim notification.
Name of the professional indemnity insurance carrier to whom the claim has been notified.
Timestamp when this indemnity claim record was last updated in the system.
Free-text field for additional notes, observations, or context regarding the claim, its handling, or its resolution.
Date on which the claim was first notified to the firms risk management or compliance team. Critical for SRA reporting and policy coverage determination.
Insurance policy year during which the claim was notified or the incident occurred, depending on policy terms (claims-made vs occurrence basis).
Financial reserve amount set aside by the insurer or the firm to cover the anticipated cost of settling or defending the claim. Updated as the claim progresses.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the reserve amount.
Date on which the reserve amount was last reviewed and updated by the insurer or claims handler.
Assessment of the risk level and potential impact of this claim on the firms reputation, finances, and regulatory standing.
Final settlement amount paid to resolve the claim, including damages, costs, and interest. Null if the claim has not been settled.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the settlement amount.
Date on which the claim was formally settled and payment was made or agreed.
Date on which the claim was reported to the Solicitors Regulation Authority, if applicable.
Indicates whether this claim meets the threshold for mandatory reporting to the Solicitors Regulation Authority under professional indemnity insurance rules.
Unique identifier for the professional indemnity insurance policy record.
Foreign key to service.practice_area.practice_area_id
The total annual premium paid or payable for this professional indemnity insurance policy.
Primary email address for the insurance broker handling this policy.
Primary telephone number for the insurance broker handling this policy.
Name of the insurance broker or intermediary who arranged the professional indemnity policy.
Indicates whether the policy operates on a claims-made basis (true) or occurrence basis (false), determining when coverage is triggered.
Email address to which claims and potential claims must be notified under the terms of this policy.
Telephone number for notifying claims and potential claims to the insurer.
Indicates whether a certificate of compliance or certificate of insurance has been issued confirming the policy meets regulatory minimum requirements.
The date on which the professional indemnity insurance coverage expires or terminates.
The date on which the professional indemnity insurance coverage becomes effective and binding.
Geographic scope of coverage defining where claims arising from professional services will be covered (e.g., worldwide, UK only, EU, excluding USA).
The timestamp when this professional indemnity policy record was first created in the system.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for all monetary amounts in this policy (limits, excess, premium).
The deductible or self-insured retention amount that the firm must pay before the insurers coverage applies to a claim.
Summary of key exclusions, limitations, and circumstances under which coverage does not apply.

Number of months of extended reporting period (tail coverage) included in the policy, allowing claims to be reported after the policy expiration date for incidents that occurred during the coverage period.
Legal name of the insurance company providing the professional indemnity coverage.
Internal reference number assigned by the insurer for tracking and correspondence purposes.
Indicates whether this policy record is currently active and in force.
The timestamp when this professional indemnity policy record was last updated or modified.
The date on which this policy was last reviewed for adequacy of coverage, compliance with regulatory requirements, and alignment with firm risk profile.
The maximum amount the insurer will pay for all claims and associated costs during the policy period, representing the aggregate coverage limit.
The maximum amount the insurer will pay for any single claim or related series of claims, representing the per-occurrence limit.
The date on which the next premium payment is due for this policy.
The scheduled date for the next review of this policys adequacy and compliance.
Additional notes, comments, or special conditions related to this professional indemnity insurance policy.
Reference identifier or document management system location for the full policy wording, schedule, and endorsements.
The externally-known unique policy number assigned by the insurer for this professional indemnity insurance policy.
Current lifecycle status of the professional indemnity insurance policy.
Classification of the professional indemnity policy indicating whether it is primary coverage, excess layer, run-off coverage for closed practices, top-up coverage, or successor practice coverage.
Description of the legal practice areas and service types covered under this professional indemnity policy.
The frequency at which premium payments are made for this policy.
Current status of premium payment obligations for this policy period.
Indicates whether the insurer is a qualifying insurer as defined by the SRA or relevant regulatory authority, meeting minimum financial strength and regulatory requirements.
The date on which the policy details were filed with or notified to the regulatory authority.
Reference number or identifier for the regulatory filing or notification submitted to the SRA or other regulatory authority confirming this policy meets minimum requirements.
The date on which this policy is scheduled for renewal or replacement with a successor policy.
The date on which the renewal notice was sent to the firm by the insurer or broker.
Name of the partner or senior officer responsible for managing this professional indemnity insurance policy and ensuring regulatory compliance.
The earliest date from which claims arising from professional services will be covered under this policy, establishing the backward coverage limit for claims-made policies.

Description of the organizational scope to which the policy applies (e.g., All Partners and Employees, Client-Facing Staff, IT Department, Global Operations).

High-level business objective or purpose of the policy, explaining what regulatory or operational risk it mitigates.

Frequency in months at which the policy must be reviewed and reaffirmed (e.g., 12, 24, 36).
Risk severity rating assigned to the policy area, indicating the potential impact of non-compliance.
URL or reference link to the primary legislation, regulation, or guidance document that the policy is based on.
Policy code of the previous version or policy that this policy replaces, establishing version lineage.

Unique identifier for this policy acknowledgement record. Primary key.

Foreign key linking to the client who acknowledged the policy

Current status of the policy acknowledgement. Acknowledged indicates active acceptance, Pending indicates awaiting client response, Declined indicates client refused (may block engagement), Expired indicates acknowledgement has passed renewal date, Superseded indicates a newer version has been acknowledged.
Email address of the individual who acknowledged the policy. Used for audit trail and for sending renewal notifications.

Name of the individual at the client organization who provided the acknowledgement. For individual clients, this is the client themselves. For corporate clients, this is the authorized representative.
Date when the client formally acknowledged and accepted the policy. This is the audit timestamp for regulatory compliance purposes.
Reference to the signed acknowledgement document stored in the document management system. Critical for regulatory audit and dispute resolution.

The method by which the client provided acknowledgement. Electronic Signature and Wet Signature provide strongest audit evidence. Implied by Engagement may be used for existing clients under legacy policies.
System timestamp when this acknowledgement record was created.
Scheduled date when the client must re-acknowledge this policy. Calculated based on the policy review cycle and regulatory requirements. Used to trigger renewal workflows.
Date when the most recent renewal reminder was sent to the client. Used to track compliance workflow progress.
Indicates whether acknowledgement of this policy is mandatory before the firm can accept new matters from this client. AML and conflicts policies are typically mandatory.
System timestamp when this acknowledgement record was last modified.
The specific version number of the policy that was acknowledged by the client. Critical for audit trail as policies evolve over time and clients must re-acknowledge new versions.

FK to compliance.data_privacy_register.data_privacy_register_id — Privacy breaches relate to specific processing activities. The breach assessment requires knowing what data categories and subjects are affected, which is documented in the RoPA entry.

Foreign key linking to document.legal_document. Business justification: Breach notifications to ICO/data subjects are legal documents: GDPR Article 33 supervisory authority notification, Article 34 data subject notification. Required for linking breach register to actual
Foreign key reference to the client matter associated with the breach, if the breach relates to client data processed in the context of a specific legal matter.

Foreign key linking to compliance.data_privacy_register. Business justification: Privacy breaches occur in the context of specific processing activities. N:1 relationship (many breaches per processing activity). Strengthens connections for data_privacy_register. No columns to remo

User identifier of the person who last modified this breach incident record. Audit trail field.

business_glossary_term: Risk Data Breach Incident Id (Foreign Key)
description: Foreign key linking to risk.data_breach_incident. Business justification: Privacy breaches (GDPR/ICO perspective) and data breach incidents (risk management perspective) represent the same event. Compliance record must reference canonical risk incident for unified breach re
Foreign key reference to the compliance or IT staff member assigned as the incident owner responsible for coordinating the breach response.

Comma-separated list or structured description of the categories of personal data affected by the breach (e.g., names, contact details, financial information, special category data under GDPR Article 9).
Name of the IT system, application, or platform from which the breach originated or which was compromised.

Root cause category of the breach incident. Used for trend analysis and preventive control design. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: human_error|system_failure|malicious_attack|third_party_failure|physical_loss|unauthorised_access|misconfiguration — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Detailed narrative description of the nature of the breach, including circumstances, systems affected, and how the breach occurred.
Physical or logical location where the breach occurred (e.g., office location, system name, third-party processor).
Current lifecycle status of the breach incident in the firms incident response workflow.
Classification of the breach according to GDPR Article 4(12) categories: accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data.
Date and time when the breach was successfully contained and no further data loss was occurring.
Description of immediate actions taken to contain the breach and prevent further data loss or unauthorised access.
Date and time when this breach incident record was first created in the system. Audit trail field.
Approximate or exact number of personal data records affected by the breach. May differ from data subjects count if multiple records per subject exist.
Date and time when affected data subjects were notified of the breach. Required without undue delay under GDPR Article 34(1).
Method used to notify affected data subjects of the breach (e.g., direct email, postal mail, public communication if individual contact is disproportionate).
Indicates whether direct notification to affected data subjects is required under GDPR Article 34. True if the breach is likely to result in high risk to data subjects.
Approximate or exact number of data subjects (individuals) whose personal data was affected by the breach. Required for regulatory notification under GDPR Article 33(3)(b).
Date and time when the breach was first discovered or became known to the firm. Critical for calculating the 72-hour notification window under GDPR.
How the breach was identified: through internal monitoring, external notification, audit, or other means.
Date and time when the DPO was notified of the breach incident.
Indicates whether the firms Data Protection Officer (DPO) was notified of the breach. True if DPO was notified.
Date and time when the breach incident occurred or is estimated to have occurred. This is the actual event timestamp, distinct from discovery or reporting dates.
Business-facing unique reference number assigned to the breach incident for tracking and communication purposes. Used in regulatory notifications and internal reporting.
Indicates whether a claim was filed under the firms professional indemnity or cyber insurance policy for costs related to the breach. True if claim was filed.
Reference number of the insurance claim filed in relation to the breach incident, if applicable.
Date and time when this breach incident record was last updated. Audit trail field.

The financial reserve established by the firm and/or its PI insurer to cover the estimated ultimate cost of the claim, including potential settlement, defence costs, and associated expenses. Used for financial provisioning and insurer reporting.

Reference to the practice group (e.g., Corporate, Litigation, IP) within which this risk originates or is managed. Enables risk aggregation and reporting by practice area for the Risk Committee.

Reference to the timekeeper or workforce member who is accountable for managing and monitoring this risk item. The risk owner is responsible for implementing mitigations and reporting status to the Risk Committee.

The date on which the risk was formally closed, either because it was fully mitigated, accepted, or the risk event is no longer applicable. Null for open risks.
